quizQuestion: Identify this anatomical structure.
explanation: This is the abdominal aorta — the section of the aorta below the diaphragm that supplies blood to the abdominal organs. It begins at the aortic hiatus (T12) and bifurcates into the common iliac arteries at L4.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of the abdominal aorta and its branches

quizQuestion: What is the large vessel shown in this illustration?
explanation: This is the aorta — the largest artery in the body, arising from the left ventricle. It carries oxygenated blood and distributes it to the systemic circulation via its many branches.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of the full aorta from heart to iliac bifurcation

quizQuestion: Name the valve structure depicted here.
explanation: This is the aortic valve — the semilunar valve between the left ventricle and the aorta. It has three cusps and prevents backflow of blood into the left ventricle during diastole. This image depicts aortic regurgitation.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of the aortic valve showing regurgitation

quizQuestion: Which body system is illustrated here?
explanation: This is the arterial system — the network of arteries that distributes oxygenated blood from the heart to the body's tissues. Arteries have thick muscular walls to withstand high pressure from cardiac output.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of the full body arterial system

quizQuestion: What does this full-body illustration depict?
explanation: This is the arterial circulation — the full-body distribution of arteries carrying oxygenated blood. The aorta branches into successively smaller arteries reaching every organ and tissue.
visualDescription: Medical illustration showing full body arterial distribution

quizQuestion: Name the structure depicted here.
explanation: This shows the artery wall layers — tunica intima (inner endothelium), tunica media (smooth muscle), and tunica adventitia (outer connective tissue). The thick media gives arteries their elastic, pressure-resistant properties.
visualDescription: Cross-section illustration of an artery showing three wall layers

acceptableAlternatives: Deep veins of the lower limb
quizQuestion: Which part of the cardiovascular system is this?
explanation: This is the deep venous system — the network of deep veins in the lower limbs that carries deoxygenated blood toward the heart. Deep vein thrombosis (DVT) can occur in these vessels.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of deep veins of the lower extremities

quizQuestion: What organ is shown in this illustration?
explanation: This is the heart — the muscular organ that pumps blood through the circulatory system. It weighs 250–350 g, beats ~100,000 times per day, and is located in the mediastinum tilted slightly to the left.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of the external heart

quizQuestion: Identify this anatomical structure.
explanation: This is the cardiac conduction system — the electrical pathway that coordinates heartbeats. It includes the SA node (pacemaker), AV node, Bundle of His, bundle branches, and Purkinje fibers.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of the heart's electrical conduction pathway

quizQuestion: What does this cross-sectional illustration show?
explanation: This is a cross-section of the heart showing the four chambers (right/left atria and ventricles) and the valves between them. The interventricular septum separates the two ventricles.
visualDescription: Cross-sectional medical illustration of the heart's internal chambers

quizQuestion: Name the structure depicted here.
explanation: This four-chamber view of the heart shows the right atrium and ventricle (pulmonary circuit) on the right, and the left atrium and ventricle (systemic circuit) on the left. The left ventricle has a thicker wall due to higher pressure demands.
visualDescription: Four-chamber cross-sectional illustration of the heart

quizQuestion: Which part of the cardiovascular system is this?
explanation: This shows the heart wall layers — the outer epicardium (visceral pericardium), the thick muscular myocardium, and the inner endocardium lining the chambers. The pericardial sac surrounds the heart.
visualDescription: Illustration of the three layers of the heart wall

quizQuestion: Identify this anatomical structure.
explanation: This is the myocardium — the thick muscular middle layer of the heart wall composed of cardiac muscle cells. It generates the contractile force needed to pump blood, and is thickest in the left ventricle.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of the myocardial muscle layer

quizQuestion: What structures are shown in this illustration?
explanation: This shows the four heart valves: the tricuspid (right AV), mitral/bicuspid (left AV), pulmonary semilunar, and aortic semilunar valves. They ensure one-way blood flow through the cardiac chambers.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of all four heart valves viewed from above

quizQuestion: Name the vascular structure depicted here.
explanation: These are the coronary arteries — the blood vessels supplying oxygen and nutrients to the heart muscle. The left coronary artery (LCA) and right coronary artery (RCA) arise from the aortic root just above the aortic valve.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of coronary artery distribution on the heart surface

quizQuestion: Identify this blood vessel structure.
explanation: This is a vein — a blood vessel that returns deoxygenated blood to the heart. Veins have thinner walls and larger lumens than arteries, and contain one-way valves (especially in the limbs) to prevent backflow.
visualDescription: Cross-sectional illustration of a vein showing its wall layers and valves

quizQuestion: What digestive process is illustrated here?
explanation: This illustrates bile secretion — bile is produced by hepatocytes in the liver, stored and concentrated in the gallbladder, and released into the duodenum via the common bile duct to emulsify dietary fats.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of the biliary system and bile secretion pathway

quizQuestion: What organ is shown in this illustration?
explanation: This is the colon (large intestine) — the final section of the GI tract, approximately 1.5 m long. It absorbs water and electrolytes, forming solid waste. Its sections are: cecum, ascending, transverse, descending, sigmoid colon, and rectum.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of the large intestine/colon

quizQuestion: Name the structure depicted here.
explanation: This is a cross-section of the colon showing its wall layers: mucosa (with goblet cells), submucosa, muscularis externa (inner circular + outer longitudinal), and serosa. The inner mucosa lacks villi, unlike the small intestine.
visualDescription: Cross-section illustration of the colon wall layers

quizQuestion: Which body system is illustrated here?
explanation: This is the complete digestive system — the gastrointestinal tract from mouth to anus, plus accessory organs (liver, gallbladder, pancreas). It breaks down food, absorbs nutrients, and eliminates waste.
visualDescription: Full-body medical illustration of the complete digestive system

quizQuestion: Identify this part of the digestive tract.
explanation: This is the duodenum — the first and shortest segment of the small intestine (~25 cm, C-shaped). It receives chyme from the stomach, bile from the common bile duct, and pancreatic enzymes, making it the primary site of chemical digestion.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of the duodenum and its connections

quizQuestion: What organ is shown in this illustration?
explanation: This is the liver — the largest internal organ (~1.5 kg), located in the right upper quadrant. It produces bile, detoxifies blood, synthesizes plasma proteins, stores glycogen, and performs over 500 metabolic functions.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of the liver showing its lobes

quizQuestion: What pathological condition of the liver is shown here?
explanation: This shows a liver with cirrhosis — diffuse scarring (fibrosis) and regenerative nodules that replace normal liver architecture. Causes include chronic alcohol use, hepatitis B/C, and NASH. It leads to portal hypertension and liver failure.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of cirrhotic liver with nodular scarring

quizQuestion: What organ is shown in this illustration?
explanation: This is the stomach — the J-shaped muscular organ between the esophagus and small intestine. It churns food with hydrochloric acid (pH 1.5–3.5) and pepsin, producing chyme. Regions: cardia, fundus, body, pylorus.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of the stomach showing its regions

quizQuestion: Identify this endocrine gland.
explanation: This is the adrenal (suprarenal) gland — a triangular gland sitting atop each kidney. The cortex produces cortisol, aldosterone, and androgens; the medulla produces adrenaline (epinephrine) and noradrenaline.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of the adrenal gland atop the kidney

quizQuestion: Name the structure depicting adrenal zones depicted here.
explanation: This shows the adrenal gland zones from outside in: zona glomerulosa (aldosterone), zona fasciculata (cortisol), zona reticularis (androgens), and the central medulla (epinephrine/norepinephrine). Mnemonic: GFR — salt, sugar, sex.
visualDescription: Illustration of the adrenal gland cellular zones

quizQuestion: Identify this microscopic pancreatic structure.
explanation: This is an islet of Langerhans — a cluster of endocrine cells scattered throughout the pancreas. Alpha cells secrete glucagon (raises blood glucose), beta cells secrete insulin (lowers blood glucose). Type 1 diabetes destroys beta cells.
visualDescription: Microscopic illustration of a pancreatic islet of Langerhans

quizQuestion: What organ is shown in this illustration?
explanation: This is the pancreas — a glandular organ with both exocrine (digestive enzymes: lipase, amylase, proteases) and endocrine (insulin and glucagon from islets of Langerhans) functions. It lies behind the stomach in the retroperitoneum.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of the pancreas showing its head, body, and tail

quizQuestion: What does this cross-sectional illustration of the pancreas show?
explanation: This cross-section of the pancreas shows its internal structure including the main pancreatic duct (duct of Wirsung), acinar cells (exocrine), and islets of Langerhans (endocrine). The duct joins the common bile duct at the ampulla of Vater.
visualDescription: Cross-section illustration of the pancreas showing ducts and islets

quizQuestion: Identify this endocrine gland.
explanation: This is the thyroid gland — a butterfly-shaped gland in the anterior neck, wrapped around the trachea. It produces T3 (triiodothyronine) and T4 (thyroxine) regulating metabolism, and calcitonin regulating calcium levels.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of the thyroid gland showing its two lobes and isthmus

quizQuestion: What degree of burn is shown?
explanation: This is a first-degree (superficial) burn — affecting only the epidermis. It causes redness, pain, and minor swelling but no blisters. It heals within 3–5 days without scarring. A typical sunburn is a first-degree burn.
visualDescription: Illustration of skin showing first-degree burn affecting only the epidermis

quizQuestion: What degree of burn is shown?
explanation: This is a second-degree (partial-thickness) burn — extending through the epidermis into the dermis. It causes blistering, intense pain, and a wet shiny appearance. Superficial second-degree burns heal in 2–3 weeks; deep ones may need skin grafting.
visualDescription: Illustration of skin showing second-degree burn with blistering into dermis

quizQuestion: What degree of burn is shown?
explanation: This is a third-degree (full-thickness) burn — destroying both the epidermis and dermis, and possibly deeper tissues. The area appears white, brown, or black and leathery. Nerve destruction means it is often painless. Requires skin grafting.
visualDescription: Illustration of skin showing full-thickness third-degree burn

quizQuestion: What does this illustration show compared to burn injuries?
explanation: This shows normal, healthy skin with an intact epidermis and dermis. Normal skin serves as a barrier against pathogens, regulates temperature, provides sensation, and synthesizes vitamin D — contrasted with the burn depth series.
visualDescription: Illustration of normal unburned skin layers for comparison

quizQuestion: What does this full-body neurological map illustrate?
explanation: This is a dermatome map — showing the sensory distribution of each spinal nerve on the body surface. Each dermatome corresponds to a spinal level (C2–S5). Used to localize disc herniations and spinal cord injuries clinically.
visualDescription: Full-body illustration of spinal nerve dermatome distribution

quizQuestion: Identify this skin layer structure.
explanation: This is the epidermis — the outermost skin layer with 5 strata (from deep to superficial): stratum basale, spinosum, granulosum, lucidum (only in thick skin), and corneum. Keratinocytes produced in the basale migrate upward over ~4 weeks.
visualDescription: Microscopic illustration of the five epidermal layers

quizQuestion: Name the skin appendage structure shown here.
explanation: This is a hair follicle — the skin structure that produces hair. It includes the hair shaft, sebaceous gland (sebum production), and arrector pili muscle (causes goosebumps). Hair grows at ~1 cm/month in the anagen (growth) phase.
visualDescription: Illustration of a hair follicle with associated structures

quizQuestion: Which body system is illustrated here?
explanation: This is the integumentary system — comprising the epidermis, dermis, and hypodermis (subcutaneous layer), plus appendages (hair, nails, sweat glands, sebaceous glands). It is the body's largest organ system at ~2 m² surface area.
visualDescription: Illustration of skin cross-section showing epidermis, dermis, and hypodermis

quizQuestion: Identify this specialized immune cell found in the skin.
explanation: This is a Langerhans cell — a dendritic antigen-presenting cell residing in the epidermis. It detects pathogens and allergens, then migrates to lymph nodes to activate T lymphocytes. Key to contact hypersensitivity reactions.
visualDescription: Microscopic illustration of a Langerhans cell in the epidermis

quizQuestion: Identify this specialized skin cell.
explanation: This is a melanocyte — a melanin-producing cell located in the stratum basale of the epidermis. Melanin determines skin and hair color and provides UV protection. Melanocytes arise from neural crest cells and connect to ~36 keratinocytes each.
visualDescription: Microscopic illustration of a melanocyte in the stratum basale

quizQuestion: Identify this lymphoid organ.
explanation: This is a lymph node — a bean-shaped secondary lymphoid organ (0.1–2.5 cm) that filters lymph. The cortex contains B cell follicles; the paracortex has T cells. About 600–700 lymph nodes are distributed throughout the body.
visualDescription: Cross-section illustration of a lymph node showing cortex and medulla

quizQuestion: What circulatory pathway is illustrated here?
explanation: This is the lymphatic circulation — the one-way network that collects interstitial fluid from tissues as lymph and returns it to the bloodstream via the thoracic duct (left side) and right lymphatic duct. Also transports dietary fats absorbed in the intestine.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of the lymphatic circulation network

quizQuestion: Which body system is illustrated here?
explanation: This is the complete lymphatic system — including lymph nodes, lymphatic vessels, and lymphoid organs (spleen, thymus, tonsils, Peyer's patches). It maintains fluid balance, absorbs dietary fats, and houses immune cells.
visualDescription: Full-body illustration of the lymphatic system and organs

quizQuestion: Name the vessel structure depicted here.
explanation: This is a lymphatic vessel — a thin-walled vessel with one-way valves that carries lymph (filtered interstitial fluid) back to the venous system. Lymphatic capillaries are more permeable than blood capillaries, allowing protein uptake.
visualDescription: Illustration of a lymphatic vessel showing its valves

quizQuestion: What organ is shown in this illustration?
explanation: This is the spleen — the largest lymphoid organ (~150 g), located in the left upper quadrant (LUQ) behind the 9th–11th ribs. It filters blood, destroys old red blood cells, and stores platelets and monocytes. Rich blood supply makes it vulnerable to traumatic rupture.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of the spleen

quizQuestion: Identify this molecular muscle component.
explanation: This is the actin filament (thin filament) — a double-helical polymer of G-actin monomers in the sarcomere. Myosin heads bind to actin during the cross-bridge cycle, pulling the Z-discs closer together and shortening the sarcomere.
visualDescription: Molecular illustration of an actin (thin) filament

quizQuestion: Identify this type of muscle tissue.
explanation: These are cardiac muscle cells (cardiomyocytes) — branched, striated, involuntary muscle cells connected by intercalated discs. The discs contain gap junctions for electrical coupling and desmosomes for mechanical strength, allowing the heart to contract in unison.
visualDescription: Microscopic illustration of cardiac muscle cells showing striations and intercalated discs

quizQuestion: What mechanism of muscle physiology is illustrated here?
explanation: This illustrates the sliding filament mechanism of muscle contraction — myosin heads (thick filaments) bind actin (thin filaments), pivot, and pull them inward, shortening the sarcomere. ATP powers each cross-bridge cycle; calcium release from the SR triggers it.
visualDescription: Illustration of the sliding filament mechanism of muscle contraction

quizQuestion: What type of muscle tissue and vessel are shown here?
explanation: This shows smooth muscle in an arteriole wall — involuntary, non-striated muscle cells arranged circumferentially. Sympathetic stimulation causes vasoconstriction; relaxation causes vasodilation. Controls blood pressure and tissue perfusion.
visualDescription: Illustration of smooth muscle cells in an arteriole wall

quizQuestion: What does this lateral view illustration show?
explanation: This is the lateral view of the brain showing the four cerebral lobes: frontal (motor/executive function), parietal (somatosensory), temporal (hearing/memory), and occipital (vision). The lateral sulcus (Sylvian fissure) separates the temporal from the frontal and parietal lobes.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of the brain in lateral view showing the four lobes

quizQuestion: What does this medial cross-section of the brain show?
explanation: This is the medial (sagittal) view of the brain revealing the corpus callosum (connecting hemispheres), cingulate gyrus, thalamus, hypothalamus, brainstem, and cerebellum. The medial view exposes structures hidden in the lateral view.
visualDescription: Sagittal section illustration of the brain showing midline structures

quizQuestion: What vascular structure supplying the brain is shown here?
explanation: This shows the cerebral arteries — the brain receives blood from the internal carotid arteries (anterior circulation) and vertebral arteries (posterior circulation), which unite at the Circle of Willis (circulus arteriosus) at the base of the brain.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of the cerebral arterial supply and Circle of Willis

quizQuestion: Identify this part of the central nervous system.
explanation: This is the brainstem — comprising the midbrain (cranial nerves III–IV), pons (V–VIII), and medulla oblongata (IX–XII). It controls vital functions (breathing, heart rate, blood pressure), relays signals between the cerebrum and spinal cord, and contains the reticular activating system.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of the brainstem showing midbrain, pons, and medulla

quizQuestion: What does this color-coded brain map depict?
explanation: This shows brain functional zones — cortical areas mapped by function. Key areas include the primary motor cortex (precentral gyrus), somatosensory cortex (postcentral gyrus), Broca's area (speech production), Wernicke's area (speech comprehension), and visual cortex (occipital lobe).
visualDescription: Color-coded illustration of the brain's functional cortical areas

quizQuestion: What cortical mapping system is illustrated here?
explanation: This shows the cerebral cortex regions including Brodmann areas and functional regions. The cortex is the outer gray matter layer (~2–4 mm thick) with ~16 billion neurons organized into six layers, responsible for higher cognitive functions.
visualDescription: Illustration of cerebral cortex Brodmann areas and functional regions

quizQuestion: Identify this limbic system structure.
explanation: This is the hippocampus — a seahorse-shaped structure in the medial temporal lobe essential for converting short-term memories to long-term memories (consolidation) and spatial navigation. Bilateral destruction causes anterograde amnesia (Korsakoff syndrome, H.M. case).
visualDescription: Medical illustration of the hippocampus within the temporal lobe

quizQuestion: What type of cell is depicted in this illustration?
explanation: This is a neuron — the fundamental signal-transmitting cell of the nervous system. It consists of dendrites (receive signals), a cell body (soma with nucleus), axon (transmits signals), myelin sheath (speeds conduction), and axon terminals (release neurotransmitters).
visualDescription: Medical illustration of a typical neuron with labeled components

quizQuestion: Name the structure depicted here.
explanation: This detailed view of neuron structure shows the cell body (soma), nucleus, rough ER (Nissl substance), dendrites for input, and axon hillock (action potential initiation zone). Myelinated segments between nodes of Ranvier enable saltatory conduction.
visualDescription: Detailed illustration of neural cell components and internal structure

quizQuestion: Name the airway structure depicted here.
explanation: This is a bronchus — one of the two main airways branching from the trachea at the carina (T4 level) into the lungs. The right main bronchus is wider, shorter, and more vertical than the left, making it the preferred path for aspirated foreign bodies.
visualDescription: Illustration of a healthy bronchus cross-section

quizQuestion: What respiratory process is illustrated here?
explanation: This illustrates pulmonary (external) gas exchange — O2 diffuses from alveolar air across the thin alveolar-capillary membrane into pulmonary capillary blood, while CO2 diffuses in the opposite direction. The membrane is only ~0.5 μm thick for rapid diffusion.
visualDescription: Illustration of O2 and CO2 exchange at the alveolar-capillary membrane

quizQuestion: What type of gas exchange is depicted here?
explanation: This is systemic (internal) gas exchange — O2 is offloaded from hemoglobin to tissue cells, while CO2 produced by cellular respiration enters the capillary blood for transport back to the lungs. Tissue pO2 is ~40 mmHg vs arterial ~100 mmHg.
visualDescription: Illustration of O2 delivery and CO2 collection at tissue capillaries

quizQuestion: Identify this portion of the respiratory anatomy.
explanation: These are the intrapulmonary airways — the bronchial tree inside the lungs, branching from primary bronchi to secondary (lobar) bronchi, tertiary (segmental) bronchi, bronchioles, terminal bronchioles, and finally respiratory bronchioles leading to alveoli (~23 generations total).
visualDescription: Illustration of bronchi branching into bronchioles within the lungs

quizQuestion: What organ pair is shown in this illustration?
explanation: These are the lungs — paired respiratory organs in the thoracic cavity. The right lung has 3 lobes (superior, middle, inferior); the left has 2 lobes (superior, inferior) plus the lingula. Total surface area for gas exchange is ~70 m² — roughly the size of a tennis court.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of both lungs in the thoracic cavity

quizQuestion: Which part of the respiratory anatomy is shown here?
explanation: This shows the lung lobes — the right lung is divided into 3 lobes by the oblique and horizontal fissures, while the left lung has 2 lobes divided by the oblique fissure. The right middle lobe is the smallest. Each lobe receives its own lobar bronchus.
visualDescription: Illustration of right (3 lobes) and left (2 lobes) lung with fissures labeled

quizQuestion: What pathological finding in the ankle is shown here?
explanation: This shows an ankle fracture — involving the bones of the ankle mortise: the medial malleolus (tibia), lateral malleolus (fibula), and posterior malleolus (tibia). The most common mechanism is inversion injury. The Ottawa Ankle Rules guide X-ray decisions.
visualDescription: Illustration of ankle bones showing fracture lines

quizQuestion: Identify the bones shown in this upper limb illustration.
explanation: These are the arm bones: the humerus (upper arm), radius (lateral forearm, rotates with thumb), and ulna (medial forearm, forms the elbow point). The radius and ulna articulate at the proximal and distal radioulnar joints, enabling pronation/supination.
visualDescription: Illustration of the upper limb skeleton showing humerus, radius, and ulna

quizQuestion: What process of skeletal development is illustrated here?
explanation: This illustrates bone growth via endochondral ossification at the epiphyseal (growth) plate. Chondrocytes proliferate, hypertrophy, and are replaced by bone through primary and secondary ossification centers. Growth hormone and IGF-1 stimulate this process until plate closure in late adolescence.
visualDescription: Illustration of bone growth plate showing endochondral ossification zones

quizQuestion: Name the ocular structure depicted here.
explanation: This is the cornea — the transparent, avascular anterior layer of the eye that refracts 65–70% of the eye's total focusing power. Its 5 layers include the epithelium, Bowman's layer, stroma (90% thickness), Descemet's membrane, and endothelium.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of the cornea showing its layers

quizQuestion: Identify this part of the eye.
explanation: This is the iris — the colored muscular diaphragm that controls pupil diameter. The sphincter pupillae (parasympathetic) constricts the pupil (miosis) in bright light; the dilator pupillae (sympathetic) dilates it (mydriasis) in dim light.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of the iris and pupil

quizQuestion: Name the ocular structure depicted here.
explanation: This is the lens — a biconvex transparent structure suspended behind the iris by zonular fibers (zonules of Zinn). Ciliary muscle contraction (accommodation) rounds the lens to focus on near objects. Clouding of the lens is called a cataract.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of the eye lens and zonular fibers

quizQuestion: Identify the muscles shown controlling eye movement.
explanation: These are the extraocular muscles — six muscles controlling eye movement: four recti (superior, inferior, medial, lateral) and two obliques (superior, inferior). CN III (oculomotor) controls most; CN IV controls superior oblique; CN VI controls lateral rectus.
visualDescription: Illustration of the six extraocular muscles controlling eye movement

quizQuestion: What does this cross-sectional illustration of the eye show?
explanation: This is the complete eye structure — showing all three tunics: fibrous (cornea + sclera), vascular (choroid + ciliary body + iris), and neural (retina). The anterior chamber contains aqueous humor; the posterior chamber is filled with vitreous humor.
visualDescription: Complete cross-section illustration of the eyeball showing all layers and structures

quizQuestion: Identify this ocular structure.
explanation: This is the vitreous body — the clear gel (99% water with collagen fibrils and hyaluronic acid) filling the posterior chamber of the eye between the lens and retina. It maintains the eye's shape and cannot be regenerated if lost. Floaters are condensed vitreous collagen.
visualDescription: Illustration of the vitreous body filling the posterior eye chamber

quizQuestion: Identify this inner ear structure responsible for hearing.
explanation: This is the cochlea — the snail-shaped inner ear structure with 2.5 turns containing the organ of Corti. Sound vibrations transmitted from the stapes create pressure waves in perilymph, bending stereocilia on hair cells to generate auditory nerve signals.
visualDescription: Illustration of the cochlea showing its spiral structure

quizQuestion: Identify this ossicle — the smallest bone in the human body.
explanation: This is the stapes (stirrup) — the smallest bone in the body (~3 mm), the innermost of the three middle ear ossicles. It transmits vibrations from the incus to the oval window of the cochlea. The stapedius muscle (CN VII) dampens loud sounds.
visualDescription: Illustration of the stapes bone and the inner ear ossicles

quizQuestion: Identify this renal filtration structure.
explanation: This is the glomerulus — a tuft of capillaries within Bowman's capsule where blood is filtered under pressure. The filtration barrier has three layers: fenestrated capillary endothelium, glomerular basement membrane, and podocytes with filtration slits. Normal GFR ~125 mL/min.
visualDescription: Microscopic illustration of the glomerular capillary tuft in Bowman's capsule

quizQuestion: What organ is shown in this illustration?
explanation: This is the kidney — a bean-shaped retroperitoneal organ (~11 cm) that filters blood to produce urine, regulates blood pressure (renin), maintains electrolyte balance, produces erythropoietin, and activates vitamin D. Each kidney contains ~1 million nephrons.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of the kidney external view

quizQuestion: What does this cross-section of the kidney reveal?
explanation: This cross-section reveals the kidney's internal zones: the outer cortex (contains glomeruli and convoluted tubules), medulla (contains renal pyramids and loops of Henle), renal calyces (minor → major) collecting urine, and the renal pelvis draining to the ureter.
visualDescription: Cross-sectional illustration of the kidney showing cortex, medulla, and calyces

quizQuestion: What does this illustration show sitting atop the kidney?
explanation: This shows the kidney with the adrenal (suprarenal) gland perched on its superior pole. Despite the name 'suprarenal', the adrenal gland is not anatomically connected to the kidney — it has its own fascial compartment and separate blood supply from the phrenic, aortic, and renal arteries.
visualDescription: Illustration of the kidney with adrenal gland on its superior pole

quizQuestion: Identify this organ and its anatomical overview.
explanation: This is an overview of kidney anatomy showing both external features (hilum, renal capsule, cortex) and internal structures (medullary pyramids, renal columns, calyces, pelvis, ureter). The hilum is the entry point for the renal artery, vein, and ureter.
visualDescription: Combined external and internal overview illustration of the kidney

quizQuestion: Identify this functional unit of the kidney.
explanation: This is the nephron — the functional unit of the kidney. Each kidney has ~1 million nephrons consisting of: renal corpuscle (glomerulus + Bowman's capsule), proximal convoluted tubule, loop of Henle, distal convoluted tubule, and collecting duct. Together they filter, reabsorb, and secrete to produce urine.
visualDescription: Illustration of a complete nephron from glomerulus to collecting duct

quizQuestion: Name the structure depicted here.
explanation: This detailed view of nephron structure shows the distinct functional segments: the proximal tubule (bulk Na+ and water reabsorption), descending/ascending loop of Henle (countercurrent multiplier for urine concentration), distal convoluted tubule (aldosterone-regulated Na+ reabsorption), and collecting duct (ADH-regulated water reabsorption).
visualDescription: Detailed illustration of nephron segments with functional annotations
